name: Demon Subduing Pestle
description: The Demon Subduing Pestle, also known as the Vajra Pestle or Jingangchu (金剛杵), is a sacred ritual object in Chinese Buddhism and Taoism, adopted from the Vajra of Vajrayana Buddhism. It is a short, club-like weapon, often depicted with a blunt end or multiple prongs, and is considered a potent symbol of spiritual power and the ability to overcome evil. Its origins trace back to ancient India, where the Vajra represented the indestructible nature of enlightenment and the power to cut through ignorance. In Chinese mythology, the Demon Subduing Pestle is wielded by deities, monks, and exorcists to vanquish demons, dispel negative energies, and protect against malevolent spirits. The pestle is not merely a weapon; it's a conduit for divine power, amplifying the wielder's spiritual strength and intention. Possessing or wielding the pestle is believed to grant protection, ward off misfortune, and promote spiritual growth. Different forms of the pestle exist, with varying numbers of prongs representing different aspects of Buddhist teachings. The Demon Subduing Pestle remains a significant symbol in Chinese religious practice, representing the triumph of good over evil and the unwavering power of spiritual discipline. It is frequently displayed in temples and used in rituals aimed at purification, protection, and the attainment of enlightenment. The weight and feel of the pestle are said to ground the user, connecting them to the earth and amplifying their will. Its presence serves as a constant reminder of the battle against inner demons and the pursuit of spiritual liberation.
subtitle: Vanquishing evil, promoting spiritual strength
origin: Adapted from the Indian Vajra; imbued with Buddhist and Taoist power through ritual and meditation.
currentLocation: Held in temples, monasteries, and private collections across China and East Asia.

keyMyths.title: Erlang Shen's Use
keyMyths.description: Erlang Shen, the powerful deity with the third eye, is often depicted wielding a Demon Subduing Pestle to capture demons and maintain order in the mortal realm. It showcases the pestle's association with divine justice and the suppression of chaos.

keyMyths.title: Monks' Rituals
keyMyths.description: Buddhist monks use the pestle in various rituals to purify spaces, dispel negative energies, and protect against evil influences. The act of wielding the pestle symbolizes the monk's commitment to fighting inner demons and promoting spiritual well-being.

sources.author: Yu Chuntao
sources.work: The Complete Book of Chinese Symbols
sources.description: Details the symbolism of the Vajra and its Chinese adaptations.
